title: What Must Be Done To Prevent DOGE From Failing

How do you team 42? It's your skipper
here, Darius Dale, to present another
episode of Proto Pro Live. I'm joined
with my friend, our friend, a fan
favorite of our crowd and our community,
Luke Gromen, founder and CEO of Forest
for the Trees. How you doing today,
Luke?
I'm doing well, my friend. How are you,
Darius? I'm doing exceptional, man.
Appreciate you you joining us. I was
saying to you in off camera, you're one
of my favorite people to follow in our
space. And the reason for that is, you
know, I feel like
you know, you're doing a real service
for the world from the perspective of
like like a Morpheus in the Matrix, if
you will. You're sort of breaking the
Matrix and and you're offering investors
this sort of red pill versus blue pill
choice. Whereas the blue pill is the
I don't know, CPI did this and Fed's
going to do that and we rinse repeat
every month and we'll keep talking about
those same two things for the next, you
know, indefinite amount of time. And
then there's the red pill, which is,
"Hey, we're in a great an era of great
social, economic, and institutional
change. And even though we don't have
all the answers of how, you know, where
we're going to wind up, let's try to put
together a defensible, robust research
process to identify those changes and
hopefully profit from those changes as
an investor." So, I assume that's why
you named your company Forest for the
Trees. Tell me a little bit about that.
Yeah, absolutely.
And that's exactly why it is. I I was
My background is is I spent almost 20
years in institutional equity research
and then institutional equity research
sales. And it was that is a it was a
fun, awesome, wonderful world to be
involved in, particularly in the years I
was there. I I started call it 95
and um
My the firm started with a old line
Cleveland money management firm that had
a sell side business and then was an
early employee and then early partner at
Midwest Research. Um and we were early
pioneers in bottoms-up fundamental
channel check research. And what does
that mean is
When I came into the business 95, 96,
97, a research call was analysts would
cover 10 15 companies, pick up the
phone, call the IR guy, the CFO, or the
CEO, and
talk about business trends, and then
hang up the phone, write up a note, send
it out to clients, and then the sales
guys would pick up the phones, and and
you know, call clients, and and that was
how the business worked.
And we did a lot by virtue of our
location of industrial and cyclical type
names.
And this all works fine when
everything's going well.
Right? So, the economy's going well,
business is going well, pick up the
phone, CFO IR guy says, "Hey, yeah,
everything's going great."
Following 97, you have the Asian crisis.
By spring of 98, the the industrial
cyclical world is in a a severe
recession. Totally. And
what we started to find was that when
you pick up the phone and ask the IR and
CFO,
"Hey, how's the you know, how's
business?"
They don't tell you the truth when
things start to go bad. They lie.
Not necessarily out of you know, they're
bad people per se. There's you know,
some of it's human nature, some of it's
securities laws, right? As time went on,
like you couldn't you know, the the the
Reg FD was you know, you you couldn't
tell people unevenly that you're going
to miss the quarter, right? So,
by virtue of that, we ended up having
buy recommendations on two of the worst
performing stocks in the S&P 500 two
years running. Wow. Yeah, right? And so,
there was a group of people I was one of
them where we it was almost like a
it was almost like a Jerry Maguire
moment, right? With the just it's just a
mission statement, right? It was it was
just a mission statement. It was like,
"Okay, the the the rules have changed.
The environment has changed. It's a game
of adjustments. We need to make an
adjustment." And the adjustment our
research department made, led by a
couple guys in particular, they did an
unbelievable job, but they got very big
into channel check research. We were one
of the first people on in in investment
in the investing world doing this. And
so,
yes, we still talked to IR, we still
talked to CFO, CEO, we still took them
on the road, and
we were calling their suppliers. We were
calling their distributors. A lot of
them were private, so they can tell us
anything they want. There's it's it's
classic build out the mosaic. So,
yeah. By virtue of that, we found
ourselves
uh doing very good research. Uh the
business grew very fast in part, you
know, doing good research and then you
of course had sort of the
a big growth in hedge funds and sort of
alternative investing through that era.
Um very much helped our clients in the
'07-'08 time frame. We we had people
positioned that. We saw it. We knew it.
We were I mean, personally, I was
sitting 100% cash by October of '07 and
warning my dad like you know, my my
parents, my in-laws like get out. Like
it's all going to come unhinged. Banks
might fail. And they looked at me like I
was the loony bin.
They, you know, so
that was my background and I was a real
junkie for reading all this research. As
we were you're driving all of this data,
I just I would take it home on weekends
and read it. And it's fascinating. And
so, I started putting together a piece
that was
basically bottoms-up macro. So, it
basically started from, you know,
instead of sort of top-down what's CPI,
what do I think's going to happen, but
down it was
okay, I just read 100 surveys across,
you know, 30 different industries
bottoms-up and prices are starting to go
up here and there's bottlenecks here or
everyone's moving to China there or
whatever the case was.
Myself and another gentleman on the
sales desk started putting out this this
thematic sort of macro bottom-up piece
and it ended up being very widely read.
And so,
I kind of learned two things from that.
Number one, this dynamic of of I would
call almost bottoms-up aggregation
connecting up data points that I was I
was pretty good at it and I enjoyed it.
And then the other thing was was just
this
like you said, the matrix, the red pill,
blue pill. I got red-pilled really early
where it was like, "Okay." Because I've
been on straight commission my entire
career. I was 100% commission then. Wow.
I was 100% I I've been a business owner
with FTT for 10 years. So, I've been on
straight commission as a salesperson or
as a business owner my entire life. And
so, when
you know, I was getting paid, my checks
were only clearing through my firm. I
was getting paid by my clients. And if
my clients didn't do well, my children
starved. And I wasn't going to allow
that to happen. So,
you know, my first month in the
business, I'll never forget. My wife is
like, "How's the first month of
commission going?" I was like, "I made
$40 this month." She's like,
"Going to be more than that eventually,
right?" I'm like, "Eventually."
Right? So, but the point is, right? You
know, you talk about incentives and
outcomes. Oh, yeah.
When we have buy recommendations on two
of the worst-performing stocks two years
running, this is existential to me.
Like, I am going to research and going,
"What we're doing isn't working. We need
to make an adjustment." And we did as a
firm, it was great.
And so, I learned both, okay, this
connecting the dots and building up I
has a lot of demand, it's really
interesting, I love to do it. And then
this discipline of
being red-pilled. You know,
you know, you know, verify, check, you
know, understanding that when the system
has interests, the system isn't going to
tell you, "Oh, by the way, we're going
to miss the quarter." No. "Hey, hey,
how's it going, Luke? Things are great.
Making so much money." A week later, we
missed by 20%. WHAT WHAT HAPPENED? "OH,
WELL, YOU KNOW." SO, both of those
things I learned early on. That was my
background. And that really, you know,
post '08, I started doing more and more
macro as uh

getting to be a much more macro
thematic-driven world. And post 2014, I
decided or 2013, I decided I want to do
this full-time.
Parted ways amicably with my uh my
former colleagues there. Um they're
still very much a bottoms-up research
shop, and we're just kind of, you know,
okay, how are we going to product place
this as a product? So,
parted ways amicably, hung out my own
shingle in 2014, and and I I did it with
no outside investors because I wanted to
have complete creative control, Mhm.
write whatever I wanted to write. So, it
was um
it was scary as heck for that first 12
months.
It was basically, you know, giving away
product for free and hoping to to make a
difference. And and, you know, in early
2015, I sort of reached out to a lot of

research to and said, "Look, here's,
you know, we've been writing. Hopefully,
you've liked it. You know, I'm going to
shut the paywall now, and if you're
interested, you know, you can subscribe

that that people had found the work

that leads to leads to the success in

let's dovetail to why everyone's here
and I appreciate everyone for being
along on this journey with us today.
Um
something you and I are very much
passionate about is the sort of you
know, the US budget deficit. You know, I
think you and I sort of share the same
general take and correct me if I'm
wrong, but
I think you and I share the same general
take that the US is either in a slow
motion fiscal crisis or likely headed
into a fast motion fiscal crisis at some
point over an investable time horizon.
Is that fair to say? So so that's the
question now. That's that's fair to say.
And so where I guess I'll start my first
question. Where do you think we are in
that process? You know, what are some of
the mile markers that you've been paying
attention to in in recent months or
quarters or years that have sort of
triggered to say we are here and we're
probably going to be there at some point
in the future.
Yeah, it's a great question. The So for
me it's been a rolling basis and there's
different levers within that that make
the situation more acute or less acute.
Um and some of that is by
virtue of the structure of the system.
So I what I would say is kind of the
a linchpin to the whole thing or
keystone to it is is the level of the
dollar. And so
if we start there, the issue is
ultimately
foreigners have 13 trillion dollars of
dollar denominated debt plus some
unknown amount of sort of euro dollar
borrowings, etc. on top of that. So they
are short at least 13 trillion dollars
in in dollar denominated debt. Yeah.
And on the other hand, they have 57
trillion dollars of of dollar assets,
which are the cumulative aggregated
gross assets of basically recycling
deficits back into US capital markets
over decades.
Uh and then within that, that is about
22 trillion dollars net, 8 and 1/2
trillion dollars of treasuries within
that number.
And so when I say the fiscal crisis
depends on different inputs and the how
acute it is,
the biggest one is the dollar.
Because ultimately, we can kind of see
for the US, right? We know the big three
expenditures.
Um we know where they are on that. We
can touch on that in a second. But the
first thing is is that anytime the
dollar gets too high,
global growth is going to slow.
Foreigners that are short dollars are
suddenly having to sell dollar assets in
order to service effectively service
dollar debt or
basically import dollar denominated
commodities.
Uh
and they sell what they can, not what
they want to necessarily. And what they
can sell are treasury bonds. And so
every time we've seen this pattern play
out, and this really an overriding theme
of this
fiscal problem has been every time the
dollar gets too strong, we start to see
the long end of the of the treasury
curve sell off in a
uh
dysfunctional manner to certain degree.
Um
and
because our debt is so high, we can only
afford so much of an interest rate,
right? So, we can't afford much beyond
4.8, 5% on the 10-year. So,
the dollar is kind of that first
overriding sort of lever. So, basically,
that's one thing I'm watching, and
that's kind of right at a um
uh
a problematic level. Now, how do I know
it's right at a problematic level as
measured by DXY is the second thing I've
really been watching since 2016, but a
lot in the last 4 or 5 years, which is
what uh I call true interest expense as
a percent of receipts. So, receipts is
just what's the government take in in
taxes all in. True interest expense is
just the gross interest that we're
paying on the debt plus the entitlement
uh the current portion of entitlements,
the pay-as-you-go portion of
entitlements. And anytime that true
interest expense as a percent of
receipts gets to
90% or so,
um where basically we're having almost
all of our receipts just to pay the
interest and interest-like portions of
our obligations, the dollar starts to
strengthen because the US government is
basically crowding out global dollar
markets with its borrowing for these
expenses,
and then that triggers the dollar to
start moving up, which starts to trigger
the dollar short, which then triggers
foreigners to join the US government
alongside them selling treasuries.
Treasury markets start selling off, and
interest rates get to a point where the
US government can't afford, and then
Fed, Treasury, someone comes in, weakens
the dollar, injects liquidity, wash,
rinse, repeat. And so, we've gone
through this rolling period, and those
periods are compressing in terms of the
span between uh-oh, you know, rates are
too high, come in do something cuz the
Treasury markets is getting
dysfunctional.
And the the structural nature, you know,
the longer we go on, every day 10,000
more boomers turn 65. You know, so that
it rolls on, and
post-Fed rate cuts, as you know,
it's been very non-linear of the
repricing of interest. So, the interest,
you know, we've got a big refinancing
coming next year. So,
it's been getting more acute. The thing
I've been really watching in recent
months, over the last 4 months, US true
interest expense as a percent of
receipts has been 103%,
which is a staggering number,
particularly when you look at things
like,
you know, fartcoin, which has, you know,
like bigger than 40% of publicly traded
companies. So, like liquidity in markets
is so great that fartcoin can have an
$800 million market cap,
but the US government still can't cover
their interest expense plus their
entitlements on a gross basis in the
last 4 months. It's So, that tells me,
when I then layer on 7 trillion in
refine next year, I think mostly in the
first half, but I could be wrong. Maybe
it's evenly out, but
you look at that and go, "Okay, this
acute problem we've had or that this
rolling problem,
it's getting acute, and it's getting
acute right now, and it's going to get
more acute with each passing month."
Awesome. That was an amazing look. So,
you know, clearly I agree with a lot of
what you're saying, and you know, but
just in the interest of, you know,
making good content here, you know, I
want to play devil's advocate, you know,
a little bit with respect to something
you said
with respect to
interest expense, you know, so, you
know, I think the
you know, you and I probably talk to a
lot of the same people that run very
large, you know, fixed income portfolios
for, you know, some multi-trillion
dollar asset managers, and, you know,
when we have when I personally have
conversations with them, you know, they
tend to think that the
acute phase of this slow-motion or, you
know, ongoing US fiscal crisis is well
in advance of us,
you know, if at all, if we'll ever even
truly experience that in the context of
US dollar hegemony.
What's your sort of pushback to that
sort of line that sort of I would say
more traditionalist line of thinking
with respect to with respect to the US
budget dynamics?
I would say it goes back to that level
of the dollar, which is to say, you
know, it's one of these things where
people say, "Well, what's the end game?"
And I've really come to abhor the phrase
end game. I think it's less end game and
more when's the rules change, right? The
game never ends, but the rules change.
And the money's the big money's made
around those rule changes. So,
what I would say is to look back
empirically to the 2020 through 2023
time frame because I think it's very
instructive in terms of
um the deficit and the acuity of the
deficit fiscal problem. So, what do I
mean by that? Uh
deficit blew out to $3.1 trillion in
COVID. Yeah. Uh
Fed comes in.
They're doing massive QE.
Dollar bottoms out, you know, dollar
peaks going into COVID,
falls from call it February-March '20
through
I want to say May-June '21 basically in
a straight line.
Um
as the Fed grows its balance sheet
massively.
And the deficit, which peaks at 3.1
trillion in COVID,
a year later
is 1.4 trillion.
Now, what happened? How did we shrink
the deficit by over half in a year?
Well, it's simple.
US GDP grew double digits nominally.
Yeah. Inflation was 8% plus officially.
Larry Summers estimates it might have
been as high as 18% a couple years
later.
The Fed was capping yields. So, we had
significantly negative real interest
rates, significant dollar weakness.
Um and so, we were able to get take
deficit from 3.1 trillion
uh-oh to 1.4 trillion. And the true
interest expense as a percent of
receipts went from about 105%
of receipts down to like 85%. Okay.
No problem. Fed comes in says, "Okay,
let's tighten. We got to fight
inflation."
What happens from '21? You know, they
finally start hiking in early '22.
Stocks sell off, bonds sell off, real
rates go positive, DXY peaks in 3Q '22
at around 114.
And 12 months later,
you had the deficit doubled
nearly or up Well, not doubled, but it
went from call it 1.4 to about 1.92
trillion dollars.
What happened?
Dollar went up. Stock prices went down,
bonds went down, yields went up,
interest expense went up, and tax
receipts
are highly tied to asset prices
on the margin.
Um That's a loaded statement. Why why do
you say that?
I think I know the answer, but I want
you to explain that to the audience.
Sure. No, it's so you know,
I've evaluated the IRS
statements. And if you look at the IRS
data that's aggregated, uh net capital
gains plus taxable IRA distributions
alone have been running at about 200%
of the annual growth in personal
consumption expenditures, PCE, which is
That's just consumer spending. That's
about 65-70% of the economy. So,
those two line items alone running at
200% of PCE growth means that
mathematically if stocks fall and stay
down,
US consumer spending mathematically
cannot grow.
And if US consumer spending doesn't
grow, the US GDP cannot grow. It's 2/3
of GDP. And critically, net That's just
net capital gains plus taxable IRA
distributions. That does not include
um
incentive comp, stock options, et
cetera, because that's all taxed as
ordinary income. So, the punchline was
is
throughout 2022 I was saying the deficit
is going to blow back out because
receipts are going to come in way less
than they think because it's all on the
margin tied to stocks.
And sure enough,
the deficit with the dollar going from
89 to 114, the deficit went from 1.4
trillion
to 1.9 trillion and then last year 2.1
trillion. So,
all by way of background of saying to
people
you know, the bond funds etc. Like the
fiscal crisis is a function of the
dollar
and ultimately of liquidity
and ultimately
I think it's important the way I have
dealt with it in terms of writing about
it and pivot points within in research
for clients
has been from a starting point of the
United States government is not going to
allow the Treasury market to dis- to
defund it.
Push comes to shove, it's not going to
allow an auction to fail.
It's not going to allow interest expense
to crowd out defense and entitlements.
And therefore, anytime the dollar gets
too strong that you're creating this
Treasury market dysfunction,
they're going to come in with more
liquidity.
And paradoxically,
what's good for
liquidity and inflation is also good for
bonds because of the structural setup,
at least initially, right? So, you would
think a weaker dollar and higher
inflation 6 months from now would be bad
for bonds, but it isn't. It's actually
good for bonds. And taking away dollar
liquidity would think is good for bonds,
it's been bad for bonds at the long end.
So, that's what I would I would say to
people is and that's why I abhor so much
this this view the end game. Well,
what's the end game, right? Well,
you know, we saw this in 3Q23 where we
had dollar up, went to 107, bonds are
selling off every day,
you know, uh stocks are selling off
every basically everything up with the
dollar or actually everything down with
the dollar, excuse me.
And we started to get that treasury
dysfunction again. We saw 30-year
auction in in
I think it was November last year at
like a five basis point tail. It was
like Yeah, huge.
You're not quite you know, you know,
huge tail. And what happened? Yellen's
like, "No problem. I'll just move it all
to the front end." Liquidity, boom. And
you know, that's where it gets to the
point as you said before the red pill or
blue pill, right? Where
sort of the traditionalists are like,
"That's not QE or the BTFP earlier.
That's not QE. It's not technically
inflationary. Blah blah blah."
And I'm like Tommy Lee Jones with you
know in in The Fugitive, you know, where
Harrison Ford's pointing his gun at him
and you know, "I didn't kill my wife."
He's like, "I don't care." Like I That's
not QE. I'm like, "I don't care." Like
it's going to reverse the dynamics that
were causing stocks to go down, which
was the bond market dysfunctioning. The
bond market is going to stop
dysfunctioning with this liquidity
injection, which this is. And when that
happens, stocks are going to go up, bond
yields are going to go down, the
dollar's going to go down, gold's going
to go up, Bitcoin's going to go up.
I don't care. Like all I care about's
making money for my clients. And so,
that's what I would say is there's this
you know, now it's been a process. There
will come a point where like either way
like the Fed gets to choose, do I want
to lose the long end by injecting
liquidity or do I want to lose the long
end by not injecting liquidity, right?
Tightening or cut. But we're not there
yet. But that to me is like the I agree
with that assessment. Like we're not
quite at that point yet, but we're
getting closer to that point at an
accelerating rate.
Yeah, no. I I would have to completely
agree with a lot of what you just said.
One, you're you're dating the heck out
of us with that movie reference.
I appreciate it. But now we're going to
be labeled as boomers by the the crypto
kids. But
uh, the uh, to the to to to to the point
on about the um, about how this sort of
this is all playing out in an
accelerated fashion. Uh, Colin, if you
put up a slide 107 from our uh, most
recent uh, macro strategy report here on
the screen, uh, we show uh, various um,
sort of the the the the we break down
the the marketable treasury market into
various investor cohorts, uh, starting
with the Fed whose share has been
declining over the past couple of years,
obviously, as they've done balance sheet
run-off. Uh, we're 15% of the total
marketable market in terms of that
cohort. Uh, we are right around 15% as
well for commercial banks. Uh, their
share had been declining as well. It
sort of bottomed uh, in 2020 uh, late
2023 as programs like the BTFP and this
sort of expectation that the Federal
Reserve was going to push through
interest rates regardless of how
nominally hot the economy uh, was really
started to um, started to uh, increase
their exposure to the market on top of
all the uh, short-end issuance from
Yellen as well. And then you've had the
foreign central banks, uh, the black
line in this chart, which is down at
14%. That's been declining essentially
every year since um, since uh, 2008. And
so the residual of all those three is
us, the private non-bank sector
globally. We went from 36% of the
marketable treasury market in late 2021
to 55% currently. And the reason that
matters, you know, that you know, none
of these thresholds is a is a magic line
in the sand, but what it ultimately
tells us is that now that we own the
lion's share of the market, us, the
global investor community, we will
naturally demand higher yields to
capitalize Uncle Sam because we're not
price insensitive borrowers like these
guys. The Fed buys for to to implement
monetary policy. The commercial banks
buy because of regulatory reasons.
Foreign central banks buy because they
need there was some place to recycle
their their their dollar reserves. We
want a unit of return as investors to
take risk in our portfolios. And so
ultimately,
in our in our opinion, and I think it
sounds like we share this opinion, this
the the movie has sped up. We are now in
a sort of more acute phase of this
process because of the fact that that
the ownership of the treasury market has
changed so much. I assume you you're
shaking your head, you're nodding your
head because you I think you agree. Is
that correct?

your views on this, Luke. Uh the reason
that I'll such a critical linchpin on
this is because the grow the dollar is
sort of inversely move inversely
correlated to the growth of the global
private balance sheet essentially. You
know, at 60% of across border bank
lending, you'd need to be less than 50%
for that to to to to be positively
correlated. At 70% of the national bond
market, you need to be less than 50% to
be positively correlated. And then you
have uh at 79% of trade invoicing. So,
the incremental units of balance sheet
capacity around the world tend to come
when the dollar is declining in price.
That tends to

profit growth, the the the household
sector uh savings growth whenever you
have and global growth broadly whenever

continue to skyrocket when you look at

called aggregate marketable uh next 12
month Treasury debt supply. And that's
really just a function of the uh debt
that's maturing in the next 12 months.

it's it's annualized really high right
now, but you know, and but we also know
the annualized rate of divestment from
the Fed's Treasury portfolio. And when
you put those three things together, the
sum of that is somewhere around $13
trillion. Now again, we know we're not
going to annualize at 3.7% in terms of
the budget deficit. It's probably fall
back down to somewhere, you know, right
around two two two trillion dollars. And
so, you can maybe haircut that $13
trillion by one to two trillion dollars,
but we're still talking about an
all-time high of roughly $11 trillion of
gross borrowing need by the US
government. And if that were the that
that we know that's coming. At minimum
$11 trillion. We know it's coming. And
so, just by sheer nature of refinancing
from a lower interest rate portfolio to
a higher interest rate market
environment, you're talking about an
additional hundred plus billion dollars
of incremental gross issuing or net net
interest expense for the US government.
So,
for the world to keep up with this
almost requires a weak dollar. And the
mere fact that we don't have a weak
dollar tells you that this situation is
getting more acute by the minute. And
the final thing I'll say on all this is
you have to be concerned about that. You
know, maybe not early next year when

uh the debt ceiling moratorium on

the non the the US exceptionalism, the

gets less dovish. Obviously, the Bank of
People's Bank of China getting
incrementally dovish. Swiss National
Bank obviously getting incrementally
dovish. The Canadian Bank 50 basis point
rate cut last week. You know, the rest
of the world is starting to move in a
way from a monetary policy making
standpoint. That almost implies wherever
the level of the dollar we settle out at
during the TGA spin down will probably
be a higher low. And we're probably
going to be off to the races on the
other side of that. If you think about

macro global liquidity proxy metric on a
year-over-year rate of change basis as

may be unfamiliar, our global liquidity
proxy is the global central bank balance
sheet plus global broad money supply
plus global FX reserves minus gold with
the bottom marker volatility overlay to
simulate the sort of impact of changes
in the repo market on on liquidity. And
we're sort of headed into a period where

of GDP. So, paradoxically,

points something doesn't seem like much,

regressive tax on the household sector
that we're giving to the corporate
sector in the form of corporate profits.

down for private sector employee, it's

means is that everything else that the

untenability. Tom McClellan had a great

our clients, which is if you go back to
1933,
anytime United States tax receipts all
in have gone above 18% of GDP,
the US has gone into recession. Even in
2022,
we didn't have an official recession,
but they got above 18% and we had two
straight quarters of GDP decline. Now,
remember what I just said about GDP
decline. If you have GD any policy that
America chooses that causes GDP to
decline is going to send the deficit up
as a percent of GDP by somewhere between
600 and 1200 basis points of GDP. So
your your deficit will explode higher
non-linearly paradoxically on any policy
that drives
a recession. So
to answer your question,
you just ran through all the math.
Nothing to cut. Yeah.
We're basically at 18% on tax hikes. Oh
boy. Can't you you were you know stone's
throw. You can't increase taxes without
causing a recession that will cause a
deficit to paradoxically rise rather
than fall.
And so what is there to do?
The only thing that's left to do
is devalue the dollar. Full stop. That's
the only thing you can do.
You can't cut without causing a
recession. You can't tax without causing
a recession.

there's probably not enough stuff to cut
from the side of a populist president.

they cannot cut compounding at 13%. So,

going to do?
People forget these second derivative
effects. You know, we I can highlight
another example beyond Obamacare of the
cuts of of efficiency, right? Sending
American jobs and factories to China
under WTO was absolutely 100%
efficiency. It reduced labor costs, it
reduced factory costs, environmental
costs. It took 10% of manufacturing jobs
offline basically within 2 years. We
papered over that for a time by giving
them subprime mortgage loans and home
equity lines to buy bass boats and
houses and then that blew up and it
ended up on the Fed's balance sheet 5
years later, 6 years later. Yeah. Uh
same dynamic here.
All these government workers, healthcare
administrators, etc. that will that
touch that 25% of federal GDP, they all
have mortgages, they have house you
know, car loans, student loans. And when
they lose their jobs, they aren't going
to get hired on as welders the next day
making 200 grand. There is a lag, there
is a retraining. And and we also learned
by the way here in the Midwest, a lot of
times there is never a retraining. What
there is is an epidemic of ODs and
alcohol and suicides, sadly. And so
that dynamic again, I don't think is by
those people just pushing the cuts.

government employees stop making their
mortgage. And then the Fed comes in and

did it.

restructure, then you have the balance

efficiency, but efficiency You fire
someone today, they stop paying the
mortgage tomorrow. Yeah. And the
efficiency benefit doesn't show up for
12 to 24 months, minimum.

balance sheet capacity, that runway of
12 to 24 months,

bank crisis
